learn.title: What's really going on here?
learn.html:
At B2 the choice between present simple and present continuous is not just "habit vs now". It reflects how the speaker frames the situation.

We use the present simple for actions, events and states seen as general, permanent or complete-as-a-fact — with no reason to think of them as temporary. We use the present continuous for situations we present as temporary, in progress, or developing around now.

Trees lose their leaves in autumn.general truth → simple
Look — the tree is losing its leaves early this year.temporary, this year → continuous

learn.title: Form — quick recap
learn.html:
Simple: base form; add -s in the third person (he lives). Spelling: watch→watches, cry→cries, go→goes. The 3rd-person -s carries no meaning but is obligatory — learners drop it even at high levels.

Continuous: am/is/are + -ing (She is working). Negatives and questions use the same be form: Are you listening? · She isn't working.

learn.title: State (stative) verbs → normally simple
learn.html:
Some verbs describe states rather than actions, and are normally used in the simple even when the state is temporary:

  • existence: be, exist
  • mental states: believe, doubt, know, realise, recognise, suppose, think, understand
  • wants & likes: want, like, love, hate, need, prefer
  • possession: belong, have, own, possess
  • senses: feel, smell, taste; appearance: appear, look, seem

I don't understand. · This belongs to me. With perception verbs we usually prefer can: Can you hear that? For possession, have got is a very common alternative: I haven't got any sisters.

learn.title: Special uses of the present simple
learn.html:
Beyond habits and facts, the simple has uses learners often miss:

  • Performative verbs — saying = doing: I promise I'll help. · I pronounce you husband and wife.
  • Historic / narrative present for immediacy in stories & jokes: So this man walks into a bar and takes out …
  • Headlines & commentary: Floods leave hundreds homeless. · Silva passes to Kane, who shoots…
  • Timetabled future: The train leaves at 9. · The course starts in May.

learn.title: What the continuous adds
learn.html:
The continuous frames a situation as bounded in time — happening around now, temporary, or changing:

  • In progress / temporary: She's working from home this week.
  • Changing & developing states (become, grow, get, improve, rise…), even if not "temporary": Prices are rising. · The climate is changing.
  • Future arrangements: I'm seeing the dentist at four.

learn.title: Typical learner errors & register
learn.html:
✗ I am knowing the answer. → ✓ I know the answer. (state verb)

✗ She works from home this week. (temporary → needs continuous) → ✓ She's working from home this week.

Overusing the continuous sounds unnatural; overusing the simple for temporary situations sounds flat. Choose by how you frame the situation, not by a mechanical rule.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Simple = general / permanent / a completed fact; continuous = temporary / in progress / developing.
  • State verbs stay simple (believe, know, want, belong…); perception often uses can.
  • Simple also covers performatives, narrative present, headlines, timetables.
  • Continuous also covers changing states and fixed future arrangements.

ru.title: Что здесь на самом деле происходит?
ru.html:
На B2 выбор между present simple и present continuous — не просто «привычка vs сейчас». Он отражает как говорящий подаёт ситуацию. Simple — общее, постоянное, факт как завершённое целое; continuous — временное, в процессе, меняющееся сейчас.

Trees lose their leaves in autumn (общая истина → simple). The tree is losing its leaves early this year (временно → continuous).

kk.title: Мұнда шын мәнінде не болып жатыр?
kk.html:
B2 деңгейінде present simple мен present continuous арасындағы таңдау — тек «әдет vs қазір» емес. Бұл сөйлеуші жағдайды қалай көрсеткісі келетінін білдіреді. Simple — жалпы, тұрақты, факт ретінде аяқталған жағдай; continuous — уақытша, үдерісте, қазір өзгеріп жатқан жағдай.

Trees lose their leaves in autumn (жалпы шындық → simple). The tree is losing its leaves early this year (уақытша → continuous).

ru.title: Форма — кратко
ru.html: Simple: базовая форма; 3-е лицо +-s (he lives). Орфография: watch→watches, cry→cries, go→goes. Continuous: am/is/are + -ing. Вопрос/отрицание — через ту же форму be.

kk.title: Форма — коротко
kk.html: Simple: базовая форма; 3-е лицо +-s (he lives). Орфография: watch→watches, cry→cries, go→goes. Continuous: am/is/are + -ing. Сұрақ/болымсыз — сол be формасымен.

ru.title: State-глаголы → обычно simple
ru.html: Некоторые глаголы описывают состояние, а не действие, и используются в simple даже если состояние временное: existence (be, exist); мышление (believe, know, think, understand); желания (want, like, need, prefer); обладание (have, own, belong); чувства/вид (feel, taste, seem). С perception чаще can: Can you hear that? Для обладания часто have got: I haven't got any sisters.

kk.title: State-етістіктер → әдетте simple
kk.html: Кейбір етістіктер әрекетті емес, күйді білдіреді және уақытша болса да simple-де қолданылады: existence (be, exist); ойлау (believe, know, think, understand); қалау (want, like, need, prefer); иелік (have, own, belong); сезім/көрініс (feel, taste, seem). Perception-пен көбіне can: Can you hear that? Иелікте have got жиі: I haven't got any sisters.

ru.title: Особые случаи present simple
ru.html: Кроме привычек и фактов: performative-глаголы (I promise, I pronounce you…); исторический/нарративный present (So this man walks in…); заголовки и комментарии (Floods leave…); будущее по расписанию (The train leaves at 9).

kk.title: Present simple-дің ерекше қолданысы
kk.html: Әдет пен фактыдан бөлек: performative етістіктер (I promise, I pronounce you…); тарихи/әңгіме present (So this man walks in…); тақырыптар мен комментарий (Floods leave…); кесте бойынша болашақ (The train leaves at 9).

ru.title: Что добавляет continuous
ru.html: Continuous подаёт ситуацию как ограниченную во времени: в процессе/временно (She's working from home this week); меняющиеся состояния (Prices are rising); запланированное будущее (I'm seeing the dentist at four).

kk.title: Continuous не қосады
kk.html: Continuous жағдайды уақытпен шектелген етіп көрсетеді: процесте/уақытша (She's working from home this week); өзгеріп жатқан күйлер (Prices are rising); жоспарланған болашақ (I'm seeing the dentist at four).

ru.title: Типичные ошибки и регистр
ru.html: ✗ I am knowing → ✓ I know (state-глагол). ✗ She works from home this week (временно) → ✓ She's working… Избыток continuous звучит неестественно; simple для временного — плоско. Выбор — по тому, как вы подаёте ситуацию.

kk.title: Жиі қателер мен регистр
kk.html: ✗ I am knowing → ✓ I know (state-етістік). ✗ She works from home this week (уақытша) → ✓ She's working… Continuous-ты асыра қолдану — жасанды; уақытша жағдайға simple — жабайы. Таңдау — жағдайды қалай көрсететініңізге байланысты.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Simple = общее/постоянное/факт; continuous = временное/в процессе/меняющееся.
  • State-глаголы в simple; perception чаще can.
  • Simple: performative, нарративный present, заголовки, расписание.
  • Continuous: меняющиеся состояния, фиксированное будущее.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Simple = жалпы/тұрақты/факт; continuous = уақытша/процесте/өзгеретін.
  • State-етістіктер simple-де; perception көбіне can.
  • Simple: performative, әңгіме present, тақырып, кесте.
  • Continuous: өзгеретін күйлер, бекітілген болашақ.

activities.prompt: Sort each sentence by how the speaker frames the situation.

items.t: The kettle is boiling — turn it off.
She's working from home this week.

activities.why: Simple = a general truth, permanent job, performative or fact; continuous = temporary, in progress right now, or a developing change.

items.s: I don't understand this question.
I'm not understanding this question.
The train leaves at 9:05 tomorrow.
Prices rise steadily at the moment.

activities.why: understand is a state verb → simple; a scheduled future uses the simple; a change happening now (‘at the moment’) needs the continuous: prices are rising.

activities.before: Normally the shop opens at nine, but this week it
activities.why: a temporary arrangement for this week → present continuous is opening (contrast with the general ‘opens at nine’).

activities.after: the results and hates being interrupted. (analyse)
activities.why: an activity in progress right now → present continuous is analysing.

activities.after: into the office, sits down and refuses to speak — it was surreal. (walk)
activities.why: the narrative present makes a past anecdote vivid → present simple walks.

activities.q: “I can't meet on Thursday — I ___ a client at two, and it's already in the diary.”
why: the simple would state a habit, not a fixed plan.
Correct — a fixed future arrangement → present continuous 'm seeing.
‘will see’ sounds like a spontaneous decision, not a diary appointment.

activities.q: “Every generation ___ the language a little, but right now slang ___ faster than ever because of social media.”
why: reversed: the general truth needs the simple and the current change the continuous.
Correct — general truth ‘changes’ + a developing change now ‘is changing’.
the second clause describes a change in progress → continuous.

activities.typeLabel: Why is the present simple used here?
activities.prompt: Match each present-simple sentence to its function.

items.t: This box contains the spare parts.

activities.why: The present simple is not only for habits: it also covers schedules, performatives, headlines/commentary and stative meanings.
‘know’ is a state verb and resists the continuous → “I know the answer already.”
‘just this month’ marks it as temporary → present continuous “She is working from home”.

activities.instruction: Rewrite so the temporary, developing nature is clear:
activities.prompt: Our costs go up. (this quarter, and it's ongoing)
activities.why: a change developing around now → present continuous are going up.

activities.scene: Choose the framing that fits each situation.

steps.npc: You look busy — what's going on?

options.t: We're launching a new product, so everyone's working late this month.
options.why: a temporary project around now → continuous.

options.t: We launch a new product, so everyone works late this month.
options.why: a temporary, in-progress situation needs the continuous: are launching / are working.

steps.npc: Does your company usually work like this?

options.t: No — normally we finish at five and take proper breaks.
options.why: a general norm → present simple.

options.t: No — normally we're finishing at five and we're taking proper breaks.
options.why: a general routine → present simple: finish / take.

activities.why: Choose the present simple for facts, norms, schedules and performatives; the present continuous for temporary situations, actions in progress, and developing changes.

learn.title: When 'state' verbs go continuous
learn.html:
The rule "state verbs aren't used in the continuous" is a useful rule of thumb — but at B2 you meet the real picture. Speakers do put verbs of liking, wanting, mental states, senses and appearance into the continuous to stress that the state is temporary, deliberate, or currently in progress.

Are you wanting another drink? (right now, to a friend with an empty glass) · Sssh — I'm thinking. (the mental process, happening now)

learn.title: Same verb, two meanings
learn.html:
Many "state" verbs split into a state sense (simple) and a dynamic sense (continuous):

I think you're right. opinionI'm thinking about it. mental activity
I see what you mean. understandI'm seeing Amy tonight. meeting
It tastes salty. qualityThe chef is tasting the soup. action
He has two cars. possessionHe's having lunch. experiencing

learn.title: be + adjective: behaviour vs character
learn.html:
With be, the continuous shows temporary behaviour, not a permanent quality:

He is selfish. his characterHe's being selfish. acting that way now

You're being very quiet today. (unusual, right now) — not a claim about your personality.

learn.title: Emotion in the continuous
learn.html:
We increasingly use emotion verbs in the continuous to emphasise a current, ongoing feeling: I'm loving this weather. · How are you enjoying the course?

With always / forever / constantly, the continuous stresses repetition — often with mild irritation: He's always losing his keys!

learn.title: Where it still doesn't work
learn.html:
Some state verbs resist the continuous almost entirely: know, believe, understand, belong, own, need, prefer, mean, consist of, contain.

✗ I'm knowing him for years. → ✓ I've known him for years.

✗ This is belonging to me. → ✓ This belongs to me.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• State verbs go continuous to stress temporariness, ongoing process, or deliberate behaviour.
  • Watch meaning shifts: think / see / have / taste all change sense.
  • He's being rude = behaving; He's rude = character.
  • know / believe / understand / belong / own stay simple.

ru.title: Когда «state»-глаголы идут в continuous
ru.html: «State-глаголы не употребляются в continuous» — полезное правило-ориентир, но на B2 картина сложнее. Говорящие ставят глаголы желания, мышления, чувств в continuous, чтобы подчеркнуть, что состояние временное, намеренное или прямо сейчас в процессе. Are you wanting another drink? Sssh — I'm thinking.

kk.title: «State»-етістіктер continuous-та қашан
kk.html: «State-етістіктер continuous-та қолданылмайды» — пайдалы жалпы ереже, бірақ B2-де нақты сурет басқаша. Сөйлеушілер қалау, ойлау, сезім етістіктерін continuous-қа қояды — күйдің уақытша, әдейі немесе дәл қазір жүріп жатқанын баса көрсету үшін. Are you wanting another drink? Sssh — I'm thinking.

ru.title: Один глагол — два значения
ru.html: Многие «state»-глаголы делятся на состояние (simple) и действие (continuous): I think (мнение) / I'm thinking (процесс мышления); I see (понимаю) / I'm seeing (встречаюсь); It tastes (качество) / is tasting (действие); He has (обладание) / is having lunch (переживание).

kk.title: Бір етістік — екі мағына
kk.html: Көп «state» етістік күй (simple) және әрекет (continuous) болып бөлінеді: I think (пікір) / I'm thinking (ойлау процесі); I see (түсіну) / I'm seeing (кездесу); It tastes (қасиет) / is tasting (әрекет); He has (иелік) / is having lunch (бастан кешу).

ru.title: be + прилагательное: характер vs поведение
ru.html: С be continuous показывает временное поведение, а не постоянное качество: He is selfish (характер) / He's being selfish (ведёт себя так сейчас). You're being very quiet today (необычно, сейчас).

kk.title: be + сын есім: мінез vs әрекет
kk.html: be-мен continuous уақытша мінез-құлықты көрсетеді, тұрақты қасиетті емес: He is selfish (мінезі) / He's being selfish (қазір солай әрекет етеді). You're being very quiet today (әдеттен тыс, қазір).

ru.title: Эмоции в continuous
ru.html: Глаголы эмоций всё чаще в continuous, чтобы подчеркнуть текущее чувство: I'm loving this weather. How are you enjoying the course? С always/forever/constantly continuous подчёркивает повторение (часто с раздражением): He's always losing his keys!

kk.title: Эмоция continuous-та
kk.html: Эмоция етістіктерін дәл қазіргі сезімді баса көрсету үшін continuous-та жиі қолданамыз: I'm loving this weather. How are you enjoying the course? always/forever/constantly-мен continuous қайталауды (жиі renjumen) көрсетеді: He's always losing his keys!

ru.title: Где continuous всё же не работает
ru.html: Некоторые state-глаголы почти не идут в continuous: know, believe, understand, belong, own, need, prefer, mean, contain. ✗ I'm knowing him for years → ✓ I've known him… ✗ This is belonging to me → ✓ This belongs to me.

kk.title: Continuous әлі жүрмейтін жерлер
kk.html: Кейбір state-етістіктер continuous-қа мүлде дерлік бармайды: know, believe, understand, belong, own, need, prefer, mean, contain. ✗ I'm knowing him for years → ✓ I've known him… ✗ This is belonging to me → ✓ This belongs to me.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• State-глаголы в continuous: временность, процесс, намеренное поведение.
  • Сдвиг значения: think / see / have / taste.
  • He's being rude = поведение; He's rude = характер.
  • know / believe / understand / belong остаются в simple.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• State-етістіктер continuous-та: уақытшалық, процесс, әдейі мінез.
  • Мағына ауысуы: think / see / have / taste.
  • He's being rude = әрекет; He's rude = мінез.
  • know / believe / understand / belong simple-де қалады.

activities.prompt: Each verb can be a state (simple) or an action (continuous). Sort these uses.

items.t: The chef is tasting the soup.

activities.why: think/taste/have split into a state sense (opinion, quality, possession → simple) and a dynamic sense (mental activity, action, experience → continuous).

items.s: I'm loving this weather!
You're being very quiet today.
I'm knowing him since 2010.
This bag is belonging to me.

activities.why: emotion (loving) and temporary behaviour (being quiet) accept the continuous; know and belong resist it: I've known him…; This bag belongs to me.

activities.after: about how to word the email. (think — mental process right now)
activities.why: the mental activity in progress → dynamic 'm thinking (contrast: ‘I think you're right’ = opinion).

activities.after: difficult on purpose. (be — acting that way now)
activities.why: be + -ing shows temporary behaviour, not character → 's being difficult.

activities.after: his keys — it drives me mad! (always / lose)
activities.why: always/forever/constantly + continuous stresses annoying repetition → 's always losing.

activities.q: “I ___ Amy tonight — we arranged it last week.”
why: ‘I see’ = I understand — the wrong sense.
Correct — ‘see’ here means meet by arrangement → present continuous 'm seeing.

activities.q: “Normally she's generous, but today she ___ oddly mean about money.”
why: Correct — a temporary, out-of-character behaviour → is being mean.
‘is mean’ would describe her permanent character, contradicting ‘normally she's generous’.

activities.prompt: Sort these verbs by whether they readily accept the continuous at B2.
activities.why: enjoy, think-as-process and have-as-experience go continuous; understand, believe and own stay simple.
understand resists the continuous → “I don't understand your point.”
own is a pure state verb → “We own two flats.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to stress the ongoing feeling right now:
activities.prompt: I really like this course. (emphasise the current experience)
alts: i am really enjoying this course
activities.why: emotion in the continuous stresses a current, ongoing feeling → 'm enjoying / 'm loving.

activities.scene: Pick the natural verb form for each turn.

steps.npc: How's the new job treating you?

options.t: I'm loving it, though I'm still getting used to the hours.
options.why: emotion + ongoing adjustment → continuous.

options.t: I love it, though I still am getting used the hours.
options.why: here the ongoing feeling is better as ‘I'm loving it’, and ‘getting used to’ needs ‘to’.

steps.npc: And your manager?

options.t: Usually kind, but this week he's being unusually strict.
options.why: be + -ing = temporary behaviour.

options.t: Usually kind, but this week he's unusually strict as a person.
options.why: a temporary behaviour → he's being strict, not a permanent trait.

activities.why: State verbs go continuous to stress temporariness, an ongoing process, or deliberate behaviour; know/believe/understand/own/belong stay simple.

learn.title: What's really going on here?
learn.html:
At B2 the past isn't just “one finished thing after another”. English gives you several tools to shape a past narrative: the past simple reports a completed event as a whole; the past continuous presents an action as in progress — a background or an ongoing frame around another event.

I read the report and emailed her. two completed events → past simple
I was reading the report when she called.ongoing background interrupted by an event → continuous + simple

learn.title: Form — quick recap
learn.html:
Past simple: regular -ed (worked, studied), irregular forms (went, saw, took). Negatives/questions use did + base form: Did you go? · She didn't know.

Past continuous: was / were + -ing (I was working; they were waiting). The base verb never carries the past marker here — be does.

learn.title: What the past continuous does
learn.html:
• Background / setting the scene: The sun was setting and birds were singing.
  • Interrupted action: We were having dinner when the lights went out.
  • Two parallel actions: While she was cooking, he was laying the table.
  • A temporary past situation: That summer I was living in Rome.
  • Softer / more tentative: I was wondering if you could help. (more polite than “I wonder”)

learn.title: used to + infinitive — past habits & states
learn.html:
used to + base verb describes a past habit or state that is no longer true, with a clear contrast to now.

I used to smoke, but I gave up. · There used to be a cinema here. · She didn't use to like coffee.

It works with both actions (used to walk to school) and states (used to have long hair). Questions/negatives drop the -d: Did you use to live abroad?

learn.title: would + infinitive — repeated past actions
learn.html:
would + base verb also describes repeated past actions, common in nostalgic storytelling. Crucially, it does not work with states.

Every summer we would drive to the coast.repeated action ✓
We would have a big house.a state → use used to: We used to have…

In a story, would usually needs a time frame to be clear: When I was young, my grandmother would tell us stories.

learn.title: Choosing between them
learn.html:
• A single finished event → past simple: I met her in 2019.
  • A repeated action, now over → used to / would: We used to / would meet every Friday.
  • A past state, now over → used to only: I used to know him. (not would know)
  • Don't confuse used to do (past habit) with be/get used to doing (be accustomed to): I'm used to working nights now.

learn.title: Typical learner errors & register
learn.html:
✗ I was knowing her well. → ✓ I knew her well. (state verb → simple)

✗ When I lived there, I would have a dog. → ✓ I used to have a dog. (state → used to)

✗ I used to living in Spain. → ✓ I used to live in Spain. (base verb after used to)

would for past habits is slightly more literary/nostalgic; used to is neutral and safer when in doubt.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Past simple = completed event; past continuous = action in progress / background / interrupted.
  • used to = past habits and states no longer true; would = repeated past actions only.
  • States take used to, not would; use the base verb after used to / would.
  • Keep used to do apart from be used to doing (= accustomed to).

ru.title: Что здесь на самом деле происходит?
ru.html: На B2 прошлое — не просто “одно завершённое действие за другим”. Past simple подаёт событие как завершённое целое; past continuous показывает действие в процессе — как фон или рамку вокруг другого события. I read the report and emailed her (два завершённых). I was reading the report when she called (фон + событие).

kk.title: Мұнда шын мәнінде не болып жатыр?
kk.html: B2-де өткен шақ — жай ғана “бірінен соң бірі болған оқиға” емес. Past simple оқиғаны тұтас, аяқталған етіп береді; past continuous әрекетті үдерісте — фон немесе басқа оқиғаны айнала қоршаған жалғасып тұрған әрекет ретінде көрсетеді. I read the report and emailed her (екі аяқталған оқиға). I was reading the report when she called (фон + оқиға).

ru.title: Форма — кратко
ru.html:
Past simple: правильные -ed (worked), неправильные (went, saw). Отриц./вопрос: did + база (Did you go? She didn't know).

Past continuous: was/were + -ing. Признак прошлого несёт be, а не основной глагол.

kk.title: Форма — қысқаша
kk.html:
Past simple: дұрыс -ed (worked), бұрыс формалар (went, saw). Болымсыз/сұрақ: did + негізгі етістік (Did you go? She didn't know).

Past continuous: was/were + -ing. Өткен шақ белгісін be көтереді, негізгі етістік емес.

ru.title: Что делает past continuous
ru.html: Фон/сцена: The sun was setting. Прерванное действие: We were having dinner when the lights went out. Два параллельных: While she was cooking, he was laying the table. Временная ситуация: That summer I was living in Rome. Мягче/вежливее: I was wondering if you could help.

kk.title: Past continuous не істейді
kk.html: Фон/көрініс: The sun was setting. Үзілген әрекет: We were having dinner when the lights went out. Қатар екі әрекет: While she was cooking, he was laying the table. Уақытша өткен жағдай: That summer I was living in Rome. Сыпайырақ: I was wondering if you could help.

ru.title: used to + infinitive — прошлые привычки/состояния
ru.html: used to + база — прошлая привычка или состояние, которых больше нет (контраст с настоящим): I used to smoke, but I gave up. There used to be a cinema here. В отриц./вопросе -d исчезает: Did you use to live abroad? She didn't use to like coffee.

kk.title: used to + infinitive — өткен әдет/күй
kk.html: used to + негізгі етістік — қазір орындалмайтын өткен әдет немесе күй (қазірмен қарама-қайшылық): I used to smoke, but I gave up. There used to be a cinema here. Болымсыз/сұрақ — -d түсіп қалады: Did you use to live abroad? She didn't use to like coffee.

ru.title: would + infinitive — повторяющиеся прошлые действия
ru.html: would + база тоже описывает повторяющиеся прошлые действия (рассказ, ностальгия). Но не работает с состояниями: Every summer we would drive to the coast (действие ✓). We would have a big house (состояние) → We used to have… В рассказе would обычно нужна временная рамка.

kk.title: would + infinitive — қайталанған өткен әрекеттер
kk.html: would + негізгі етістік да қайталанған өткен әрекеттерді білдіреді (әңгіме, ностальгия). Бірақ ол күймен қолданылмайды: Every summer we would drive to the coast (әрекет ✓). We would have a big house (күй) → We used to have… Әңгімеде would әдетте уақыт аясын қажет етеді.

ru.title: Что выбрать
ru.html: Одно завершённое событие → past simple: I met her in 2019. Повторявшееся, но уже нет → used to / would. Прошлое состояние, уже нет → только used to: I used to know him (не would). Не путайте used to do (прошлая привычка) и be/get used to doing (быть привыкшим): I'm used to working nights now.

kk.title: Қайсысын таңдау керек
kk.html: Бір аяқталған оқиға → past simple: I met her in 2019. Қайталанған, енді жоқ әрекет → used to / would. Өткен күй, енді жоқ → тек used to: I used to know him (would емес). used to do (өткен әдет) мен be/get used to doing (үйреніп қалу) шатастырмаңыз: I'm used to working nights now.

ru.title: Типичные ошибки и регистр
ru.html: ✗ I was knowing her → ✓ I knew her (состояние → simple). ✗ When I lived there, I would have a dog → ✓ I used to have a dog (состояние → used to). ✗ I used to living → ✓ I used to live (после used to — база). would — более литературно/ностальгически; used to — нейтрально и безопаснее.

kk.title: Жиі қателер мен регистр
kk.html: ✗ I was knowing her → ✓ I knew her (күй → simple). ✗ When I lived there, I would have a dog → ✓ I used to have a dog (күй → used to). ✗ I used to living → ✓ I used to live (used to-дан кейін негізгі етістік). would — әдебирек/ностальгиялық; used to — бейтарап әрі қауіпсіз.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Past simple = завершённое событие; past continuous = в процессе/фон/прерванное.
  • used to = прошлые привычки И состояния; would = только повторяющиеся действия.
  • Состояния → used to, не would; после used to/would — база.
  • used to do ≠ be used to doing (быть привыкшим).

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Past simple = аяқталған оқиға; past continuous = үдерісте/фон/үзілген.
  • used to = өткен әдет ЖӘНЕ күй; would = тек қайталанған әрекет.
  • Күй used to алады, would емес; used to/would-тан кейін негізгі етістік.
  • used to do ≠ be used to doing (үйреніп қалу).

activities.prompt: Sort each verb form by what it does in a narrative.
activities.why: Past simple reports a whole finished event; past continuous frames an ongoing action or scene.

items.s: Every winter we would go skiing.
When I was a child, I would have a bike.
There used to be a market here.
I was knowing the answer.

activities.why: would works with repeated actions but not states (have → used to have); know is a state verb → knew.

activities.after: a shower when the doorbell rang. (have)
activities.why: the longer background action interrupted by an event → past continuous was having.

activities.after: long hair, but she cut it last year. (used to / have)
activities.why: a past state no longer true → used to have (base verb after used to).

activities.after: vegetables as a kid, but now he loves them. (didn't / like)
activities.why: the negative drops the -d → didn't use to like.
two parallel background actions → both past continuous.

activities.q: “We ___ along the coast road when suddenly a deer ___ out in front of us.”
why: the background needs the continuous and the sudden event the simple.
Correct — ongoing ‘were driving’ + a sudden completed event ‘stepped’.
the deer's appearance is a single completed event → past simple.

activities.q: “My grandfather ___ a small farm, and every harvest he ___ hire the whole village.”
why: own is a state → used to, not would.
Correct — ‘used to own’ (state) + ‘would hire’ (repeated action).
the second, a repeated action in a nostalgic story, fits ‘would’ well.

activities.typeLabel: used to, would, or both possible?
activities.prompt: Which structure(s) can express each past idea?
activities.why: States (believe, own) take only used to; repeated actions (walk, play) allow both used to and would.
‘have’ here is a state → “I used to have a cat when I was young.” (would doesn't work with states)

activities.instruction: Rewrite using the past continuous to give background:
activities.prompt: The sun set. We walked home.
activities.answer: the sun was setting as we walked home
alts: the sun was setting when we walked home
as we walked home, the sun was setting
activities.why: the ongoing scene becomes background → past continuous ‘was setting’.

activities.prompt: In the past, this building was a hospital, but it isn't now.
activities.answer: this building used to be a hospital
alts: there used to be a hospital here
this building used to be a hospital.
activities.why: a past state no longer true → used to be.

activities.scene: Choose the natural past form for each turn.

steps.npc: What was your village like when you were small?

options.t: Quiet. We used to know everyone, and on summer evenings we'd sit outside talking.
options.why: ‘used to know’ (state) + ‘'d sit’ (repeated action).

options.t: Quiet. We would know everyone, and on summer evenings we used to sit outside.
options.why: know is a state → used to know; the repeated ‘sit’ can take either, but ‘would know’ is wrong.

steps.npc: What happened the day you left?

options.t: It was raining hard, and as the bus pulled away I waved until I couldn't see them.
options.why: background ‘was raining’ + completed events ‘pulled away/waved’.

options.t: It rained hard, and as the bus was pulling away I was waving until I didn't see them.
options.why: the scene-setting rain is better as background: ‘was raining’, with completed ‘waved’.

activities.why: Combine the past simple (completed events) with the past continuous (background/interrupted); use used to for past habits and states, and would for repeated past actions only.

learn.title: The four past tenses as a system
learn.html:
A good B2 narrative weaves four past forms together, each with a distinct job:

  • Past simple — the main events, in sequence: She opened the letter and gasped.
  • Past continuous — background / scene: It was raining.
  • Past perfect — an earlier past (before the main events): Someone had left it on her desk.
  • Past perfect continuous — an ongoing earlier past up to a past point: She had been waiting all week.

learn.title: Past perfect — a step further back
learn.html:
had + past participle marks an event as completed before another past moment. It lets you break strict chronological order and still be clear.

When we arrived, the film had already started.the starting happened before we arrived
She told me she had seen the report.seeing was earlier than telling

It is the past equivalent of the present perfect: He has left (now) → He had left (by then).

learn.title: Past perfect continuous — duration up to a past point
learn.html:
had been + -ing stresses the length or ongoing nature of an activity leading up to a moment in the past — often to explain a result.

His eyes were red because he had been crying. · By noon they had been driving for six hours. · I was tired — I had been working since dawn.

Compare the completed result vs the activity: She had painted the room (finished) vs She had been painting (that's why there was paint everywhere).

learn.title: Sequencing words: when past perfect is (un)necessary
learn.html:
With after, before, by the time, already, the order is often obvious, so the past perfect is optional but common in careful/written English:

After she (had) finished, she left.

But when two events are simultaneous or in obvious sequence with and/then, keep the past simple: He opened the door and walked in. (not had opened)

Meaning can change: When I arrived, they left (I arrived, then they left) vs When I arrived, they had left (they were already gone).

learn.title: Putting it together in a story
learn.html:
Watch the layers combine: It was getting dark (background). Maria hurried home (main event). She had been walking for an hour (earlier ongoing) and had forgotten her umbrella (earlier completed), so by the time she reached the door she was soaked.

The past perfect forms carry the backstory; the past simple carries the plot; the continuous forms carry the atmosphere.

learn.title: Typical learner errors & register
learn.html:
✗ After I had eaten, I had gone to bed. → ✓ After I had eaten, I went to bed. (only the earlier action needs the past perfect)

✗ I had been knowing him for years. → ✓ I had known him for years. (state verb → no continuous)

Overusing the past perfect makes writing heavy. Use it only where the earlier-past relationship genuinely matters or would otherwise be unclear.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Past perfect (had + p.p.) = an event completed before another past moment.
  • Past perfect continuous (had been + -ing) = duration/activity up to a past point, often explaining a result.
  • Only the earlier action takes the past perfect; the later one stays past simple.
  • State verbs use the past perfect, not the continuous (had known, not had been knowing).

ru.title: Четыре прошедших времени как система
ru.html: Хороший рассказ на B2 сплетает четыре прошедшие формы: past simple — главные события по порядку; past continuous — фон/сцена; past perfect — более раннее прошлое; past perfect continuous — длящееся раннее прошлое до момента в прошлом.

kk.title: Төрт өткен шақ — бір жүйе
kk.html: Жақсы B2 әңгіме төрт өткен форманы біріктіреді: past simple — негізгі оқиғалар, ретімен; past continuous — фон/көрініс; past perfect — негізгіден бұрынғы өткен; past perfect continuous — өткен нүктеге дейін жалғасқан ерте өткен.

ru.title: Past perfect — ещё дальше в прошлое
ru.html: had + past participle помечает событие как завершённое до другого прошлого момента: When we arrived, the film had already started (началось до нашего прихода). Это прошедший эквивалент present perfect: He has left (сейчас) → He had left (к тому моменту).

kk.title: Past perfect — бір қадам артқа
kk.html: had + past participle оқиғаны басқа өткен сәттен бұрын аяқталған етіп белгілейді: When we arrived, the film had already started (басталу — біз келгенге дейін). Ол present perfect-тің өткендегі баламасы: He has left (қазір) → He had left (сол кезде).

ru.title: Past perfect continuous — длительность до момента в прошлом
ru.html: had been + -ing подчёркивает длительность/процесс действия до прошлого момента, часто объясняя результат: His eyes were red because he had been crying. By noon they had been driving for six hours. Сравни: She had painted the room (завершено) vs She had been painting (потому и была краска повсюду).

kk.title: Past perfect continuous — өткен нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтық
kk.html: had been + -ing өткен сәтке дейінгі әрекеттің ұзақтығын/жалғасын баса көрсетеді, жиі нәтижені түсіндіру үшін: His eyes were red because he had been crying. By noon they had been driving for six hours. Салыстыр: She had painted the room (аяқталды) vs She had been painting (сондықтан бәрі бояуда болды).

ru.title: Слова последовательности: нужен ли past perfect
ru.html: С after, before, by the time, already порядок ясен, поэтому past perfect необязателен (но част в письме): After she (had) finished, she left. Но с and/then при явной последовательности — past simple: He opened the door and walked in. Смысл может меняться: When I arrived, they left (я пришёл, потом ушли) vs When I arrived, they had left (уже ушли).

kk.title: Реттік сөздер: past perfect қажет пе?
kk.html: after, before, by the time, already-мен рет анық, сондықтан past perfect міндетті емес (бірақ жазба тілде жиі): After she (had) finished, she left. Бірақ and/then-мен қатар/анық ретте past simple қалады: He opened the door and walked in. Мағына өзгеруі мүмкін: When I arrived, they left (келдім, сосын кетті) vs When I arrived, they had left (кетіп қойған еді).

ru.title: Собираем в рассказ
ru.html: Смотрите на слои: It was getting dark (фон). Maria hurried home (главное). She had been walking for an hour (раннее длящееся) and had forgotten her umbrella (раннее завершённое), so by the time she reached the door she was soaked. Past perfect — предыстория; past simple — сюжет; continuous — атмосфера.

kk.title: Әңгімеде біріктіру
kk.html: Қабаттарды қараңыз: It was getting dark (фон). Maria hurried home (негізгі). She had been walking for an hour (ерте жалғасқан) and had forgotten her umbrella (ерте аяқталған), so by the time she reached the door she was soaked. Past perfect — алдыңғы оқиға; past simple — сюжет; continuous — атмосфера.

ru.title: Типичные ошибки и регистр
ru.html: ✗ After I had eaten, I had gone to bed → ✓ After I had eaten, I went to bed (только раннее действие в past perfect). ✗ I had been knowing him → ✓ I had known him (состояние → без continuous). Избыток past perfect утяжеляет текст — используйте только там, где связь “раньше” действительно важна.

kk.title: Жиі қателер мен регистр
kk.html: ✗ After I had eaten, I had gone to bed → ✓ After I had eaten, I went to bed (тек ерте әрекет past perfect алады). ✗ I had been knowing him → ✓ I had known him (күй → continuous жоқ). Past perfect-ті асыра қолдану мәтінді ауырлатады — тек ерте-өткен қатынасы шынымен маңызды жерде қолданыңыз.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Past perfect = событие, завершённое до другого прошлого момента.
  • Past perfect continuous = длительность/процесс до прошлого момента, часто объясняет результат.
  • Только раннее действие в past perfect; позднее — past simple.
  • Состояния — past perfect, не continuous (had known).

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Past perfect = басқа өткен сәттен бұрын аяқталған оқиға.
  • Past perfect continuous = өткен нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтық/әрекет, жиі нәтижені түсіндіреді.
  • Тек ерте әрекет past perfect алады; кейінгісі past simple.
  • Күй етістіктері continuous емес past perfect алады (had known).

activities.prompt: Sort each verb form by its role in a story.

items.t: he had been running for miles

activities.why: Past simple carries events; past perfect carries earlier background; the continuous forms carry ongoing scene/activity. (had been running counts as ongoing earlier activity.)

activities.typeLabel: Is the past perfect used correctly?

items.s: When I got home, my brother had cooked dinner.
He opened the box and had found a ring.
She was exhausted because she had been studying all night.
I had been knowing them for years.

activities.why: Only the earlier of two events takes the past perfect; sequential and/then events stay past simple; state verbs (know) don't take the continuous.

activities.before: By the time the police arrived, the thieves
activities.why: disappearing happened before the police arrived → past perfect had already disappeared.

activities.before: Her hands were covered in flour because she
activities.why: an ongoing earlier activity explaining a present-in-the-past result → had been baking.

activities.after: each other for a decade before we finally married. (know)
activities.why: know is a state verb → past perfect had known, not ‘had been knowing’.
falling asleep happened before the call → past perfect ‘had just fallen’.

activities.q: Which sentence means the guests were ALREADY GONE before I got there?
options: When I arrived, the guests left.
When I arrived, the guests had left.
When I arrived, the guests were leaving.
why: this means they left after I arrived.
Correct — the past perfect ‘had left’ places their departure before my arrival.
this means they were in the process of leaving as I arrived.

activities.q: “The garden looked wonderful — they ___ it for the party.”
why: the continuous stresses the activity, not the finished, visible result.
Correct — a completed result you can see → past perfect simple had decorated.

activities.prompt: In each pair, is the past perfect grammatically necessary, or just optional after a clear sequencer?

items.t: When I arrived they ___ left (already gone)
By the time we ___ found it, it was too late
I realised I ___ met him before

activities.why: With after/before/by the time the order is already clear, so the past perfect is optional; elsewhere it removes ambiguity about which event came first.
only the earlier action (paying) needs the past perfect → “After we had paid, we left.”

activities.instruction: Combine, making the earlier event a past perfect:
activities.prompt: I lost my keys. Then I realised it.
activities.answer: i realised i had lost my keys
alts: i realised that i had lost my keys
i realised i'd lost my keys
activities.why: losing happened before realising → past perfect ‘had lost’ in the reported clause.

activities.instruction: Explain the result with the past perfect continuous:
activities.prompt: He was out of breath. He had run to the station.
activities.answer: he was out of breath because he had been running to the station
alts: he was out of breath because he had been running
he was out of breath, having been running to the station
activities.why: the ongoing prior activity explains the result → had been running.

activities.scene: Sequence the past events correctly.

steps.npc: Why were you so upset at the meeting?

options.t: I'd prepared the wrong report — I hadn't checked the email that changed the topic.
options.why: both actions happened before the meeting → past perfect ‘d prepared / hadn't checked.

options.t: I prepared the wrong report — I didn't check the email that had changed the topic.
options.why: to make clear these happened before the meeting, use the past perfect for your earlier actions.

steps.npc: How long had it been going wrong?

options.t: For weeks. I'd been using an old template without realising.
options.why: an ongoing earlier activity → past perfect continuous ‘d been using.

options.t: For weeks. I was using an old template without realising, before the meeting.
options.why: the ongoing activity leading up to the past point → had been using.

activities.why: Layer the tenses: past simple for the plot, past perfect for what came before, and past perfect continuous for ongoing earlier activity — marking only the earlier action, and keeping state verbs out of the continuous.

learn.title: The real distinction: connection to now
learn.html:
The choice isn't about how long ago — it's about whether the speaker connects the event to the present. The present perfect presents a past action as relevant now (a result, news, experience, or unfinished time). The past simple reports an event as finished and separate from now, usually with a definite past time.

I've lost my keys.result matters now (I can't get in) → present perfect
I lost them yesterday.a finished event at a past time → past simple

learn.title: Form & signposts
learn.html:
Present perfect: have/has + past participle. Typical markers: just, already, yet, ever, never, so far, recently, since, for, this week/month/year (a period still open).

Past simple: base past form. Typical markers: yesterday, ago, last week, in 2019, then, when I was… (a finished time).

learn.title: Finished vs unfinished time
learn.html:
The same time word can trigger either tense depending on whether the period is over:

I've drunk three coffees today.today isn't over → present perfect
I drank three coffees this morning.the morning is over (it's now afternoon) → past simple

this week / this year take the present perfect while the period continues; a closed period (last week) takes the past simple.

learn.title: Experience, news & result
learn.html:
• Life experience (unspecified time): Have you ever eaten octopus? · She's visited forty countries.
  • Recent news / just now: The president has resigned. · I've just finished.
  • Present result: He's broken his leg (so he can't play).

Once you add a definite time or ask when, switch to the past simple: She visited Peru in 2018. · When did you finish?

learn.title: The news-then-details pattern
learn.html:
A very common B2 move: announce with the present perfect, then give details in the past simple.

I've had a great holiday! We flew to Lisbon, stayed in the old town and ate far too much.

The first sentence links the trip to now (that's my news); the details are finished events located in past time.

learn.title: Register & regional notes
learn.html:
In American English, the past simple is often used where British English prefers the present perfect, especially with just / already / yet: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs Have you eaten yet? (BrE).

In exams and formal writing, the British distinction is expected — so keep the present perfect for present relevance and unfinished time.

learn.title: Typical learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I have seen him yesterday. → ✓ I saw him yesterday. (definite past time → past simple)

✗ When have you arrived? → ✓ When did you arrive? (asking when → past simple)

✗ I live here since 2020. → ✓ I've lived here since 2020. (unfinished time up to now → present perfect)

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Present perfect = past action connected to now (result, news, experience, unfinished time).
  • Past simple = finished event at a definite past time.
  • A definite time or when? forces the past simple.
  • Announce in the present perfect, give details in the past simple.

ru.title: Настоящее различие: связь с сейчас
ru.html: Выбор не про как давно, а про то, связывает ли говорящий событие с настоящим. Present perfect подаёт прошлое как значимое сейчас (результат, новость, опыт, незавершённое время). Past simple — событие завершено и отделено от сейчас, обычно с точным прошлым временем. I've lost my keys (важен результат) vs I lost them yesterday (завершено).

kk.title: Нақты айырма: қазіргімен байланыс
kk.html: Таңдау қашан болғанында емес — сөйлеуші оқиғаны қазіргімен байланыстыра ма, соған байланысты. Present perfect өткен әрекетті қазір өзекті етіп береді (нәтиже, жаңалық, тәжірибе, аяқталмаған уақыт). Past simple — оқиға аяқталған әрі қазірден бөлек, әдетте нақты өткен уақытпен. I've lost my keys (нәтиже маңызды) vs I lost them yesterday (аяқталған).

ru.title: Форма и сигналы
ru.html: Present perfect: have/has + past participle. Маркеры: just, already, yet, ever, never, so far, recently, since, for, this week/year (открытый период). Past simple: yesterday, ago, last week, in 2019, when I was… (завершённое время).

kk.title: Форма мен белгілер
kk.html: Present perfect: have/has + past participle. Белгілер: just, already, yet, ever, never, so far, recently, since, for, this week/year (ашық кезең). Past simple: yesterday, ago, last week, in 2019, when I was… (аяқталған уақыт).

ru.title: Завершённое vs незавершённое время
ru.html: Одно и то же слово времени выбирает время в зависимости от того, закончился ли период: I've drunk three coffees today (today не закончился → perfect) vs I drank three coffees this morning (утро прошло → simple). this week/year — пока период идёт, perfect; last week — simple.

kk.title: Аяқталған vs аяқталмаған уақыт
kk.html: Бірдей уақыт сөзі кезеңнің бітуіне қарай екі шақты да таңдай алады: I've drunk three coffees today (today бітпеген → perfect) vs I drank three coffees this morning (таң бітті → simple). this week/year — кезең жалғасса perfect; last week — simple.

ru.title: Опыт, новости, результат
ru.html: Жизненный опыт (без точного времени): Have you ever eaten octopus? She's visited forty countries. Свежая новость: The president has resigned. I've just finished. Результат сейчас: He's broken his leg. С точным временем или when → past simple: She visited Peru in 2018. When did you finish?

kk.title: Тәжірибе, жаңалық, нәтиже
kk.html: Өмір тәжірибесі (нақты уақытсыз): Have you ever eaten octopus? She's visited forty countries. Жаңа жаңалық: The president has resigned. I've just finished. Қазіргі нәтиже: He's broken his leg. Нақты уақыт немесе when қосылса → past simple: She visited Peru in 2018. When did you finish?

ru.title: Модель «новость — потом детали»
ru.html: Частый приём B2: объявить в present perfect, затем детали в past simple: I've had a great holiday! We flew to Lisbon, stayed in the old town and ate too much. Первое предложение связывает поездку с настоящим; детали — завершённые события в прошлом.

kk.title: «Жаңалық — сосын егжей-тегжей» үлгісі
kk.html: Жиі B2 тәсілі: present perfect-пен хабарлап, сосын past simple-де егжей-тегжейін беру: I've had a great holiday! We flew to Lisbon, stayed in the old town and ate too much. Бірінші сөйлем сапарды қазірмен байланыстырады; қалғаны — өткендегі аяқталған оқиғалар.

ru.title: Регистр и региональные пометы
ru.html: В американском английском с just/already/yet часто past simple: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs Have you eaten yet? (BrE). На экзаменах и в формальном письме ждут британское различие — используйте present perfect для актуальности и незавершённого времени.

kk.title: Регистр және аймақтық ескертпе
kk.html: Американдық ағылшында just/already/yet-пен жиі past simple қолданылады: Did you eat yet? (AmE) vs Have you eaten yet? (BrE). Емтихан мен ресми жазуда британдық айырма күтіледі — қазіргі өзектілік пен аяқталмаған уақытқа present perfect сақтаңыз.

ru.title: Типичные ошибки
ru.html: ✗ I have seen him yesterday → ✓ I saw him yesterday (точное прошлое). ✗ When have you arrived? → ✓ When did you arrive? (when → simple). ✗ I live here since 2020 → ✓ I've lived here since 2020 (незавершённое время до сейчас).

kk.title: Жиі қателер
kk.html: ✗ I have seen him yesterday → ✓ I saw him yesterday (нақты өткен уақыт). ✗ When have you arrived? → ✓ When did you arrive? (when → simple). ✗ I live here since 2020 → ✓ I've lived here since 2020 (қазірге дейінгі аяқталмаған уақыт).

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Present perfect = прошлое, связанное с сейчас (результат, новость, опыт, незавершённое время).
  • Past simple = завершённое событие в точное прошлое время.
  • Точное время или when? → past simple.
  • Объявляйте в present perfect, детали — в past simple.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Present perfect = қазіргімен байланысты өткен (нәтиже, жаңалық, тәжірибе, аяқталмаған уақыт).
  • Past simple = нақты өткен уақыттағы аяқталған оқиға.
  • Нақты уақыт немесе when? → past simple.
  • Present perfect-пен хабарлап, past simple-де егжей-тегжейін беріңіз.

activities.typeLabel: Which tense does the time word signal?
activities.prompt: Sort each time expression by the tense it typically triggers.
activities.why: just/so far/ever point to now (present perfect); yesterday/in 2015/…ago are finished times (past simple).

items.s: I've been to Japan twice.
I have gone there last summer.
She's just left the office.
When have you seen him?

activities.why: a definite time (last summer) and ‘when?’ both force the past simple: I went / When did you see…

activities.before: I can't believe how much I
activities.why: ‘today’ is still open → present perfect 've done.
‘back in 2019’ is a finished time → past simple bought.

activities.after: in that house since their wedding. (live)
activities.why: a state continuing up to now with ‘since’ → present perfect 've lived.
life experience with ‘ever’ → present perfect question.

activities.q: It's 3 p.m. Which is natural?
options: I've written five emails this morning.
I wrote five emails this morning.
I write five emails this morning.
why: the morning is over now, so it's a finished period.
Correct — ‘this morning’ (now afternoon) is finished → past simple wrote.
the present simple doesn't fit a specific completed action.

activities.q: “Great news — I ___ the job! I ___ the interview yesterday and they ___ me an hour ago.”
why: mixing the pattern up.
Correct — announce with present perfect ‘'ve got’, then details in past simple ‘had/called’.
the headline of the news is better in the present perfect.

activities.prompt: Does each period point to the past simple (finished) or present perfect (still open)?
activities.why: An open period up to now takes the present perfect; a closed period takes the past simple.
a definite past time (in 2021) requires the past simple → “She finished her degree in 2021.”

activities.instruction: Rewrite to stress present relevance (something is different now):
activities.prompt: I lost my phone. (and I still don't have it)
activities.why: an ongoing present result → present perfect 've lost.

activities.prompt: You've spoken to the manager.
activities.answer: when did you speak to the manager
alts: when did you speak to the manager?
activities.why: asking ‘when’ pins the event to a past time → past simple did you speak.

activities.typeLabel: Extended context: back from a trip
activities.scene: Choose present perfect for news, past simple for details.

steps.npc: How was your week away?

options.t: Amazing — I've just got back! We hiked every day and the weather was perfect.
options.why: news in present perfect, finished details in past simple.

options.t: Amazing — I just get back! We have hiked every day and the weather has been perfect.
options.why: announce with ‘I've just got back’, then details in the past simple: hiked / was.

steps.npc: Have you ever been there before?

options.t: No, it was my first time. I'd always wanted to go.
options.why: experience question in present perfect; the answer refers to a finished trip → past simple.

options.t: No, it has been my first time. I always want to go.
options.why: the specific trip is finished → ‘it was my first time’.

activities.why: Use the present perfect for events connected to now (result, news, experience, unfinished time) and the past simple for finished events at a definite time — announcing in the perfect and giving details in the simple.

learn.title: Result vs activity
learn.html:
Both look back from now, but they emphasise different things. The present perfect simple focuses on a completed result or achievement (often how much / how many). The present perfect continuous focuses on the activity itself — its duration or the fact that it has been going on (often how long), sometimes still continuing.

I've written three pages.a finished, countable result → simple
I've been writing all afternoon.the ongoing activity & its duration → continuous

learn.title: Form
learn.html:
Simple: have/has + past participle. Continuous: have/has + been + -ing.

Both use for and since for duration: for two hours · since Monday. Only the simple answers how many/how much (finished); the continuous answers how long (activity).

learn.title: Finished vs still happening — and visible results
learn.html:
• Continuous often means the activity is still going on or has just stopped, with a visible/present effect: You're out of breath — have you been running?
  • Simple presents the result as complete: I've cleaned the kitchen (it's done).
  • Compare: Who's eaten my cake? (it's gone — result) vs Who's been eating my cake? (some is missing — the activity/who's responsible).

learn.title: Duration: which verbs prefer which
learn.html:
For states (know, be, have = possess, like), use the simple even for duration: I've known her for years. (not been knowing)

For dynamic verbs describing an ongoing activity up to now, the continuous is natural: I've been studying English since 2018. With live / work / teach, both are common with little difference: She's worked / 's been working here for a decade.

learn.title: Nuance: attitude & completeness
learn.html: The continuous can imply the activity is temporary or emphasise effort/repetition: I've been trying to reach you all day! The simple can imply a permanent or complete reading: I've read that book (all of it) vs I've been reading that book (in progress, maybe unfinished).

learn.title: Typical learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I've been knowing him for years. → ✓ I've known him for years. (state verb)

✗ I've been finishing the report. (if it's done) → ✓ I've finished the report. (a completed result)

✗ How long have you written? → for an activity that's still going, ✓ How long have you been writing?

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Simple = completed result / how many; continuous = ongoing activity / how long.
  • Continuous often = still happening or just stopped, with a visible effect.
  • State verbs use the simple for duration (have known); dynamic activities use the continuous.
  • live/work/study allow both with little difference.

ru.title: Результат vs процесс
ru.html: Оба смотрят из настоящего назад, но подчёркивают разное. Present perfect simple — завершённый результат/достижение (часто how much/how many). Present perfect continuous — сам процесс, его длительность или что он шёл (часто how long). I've written three pages (результат) vs I've been writing all afternoon (процесс/длительность).

kk.title: Нәтиже vs әрекет
kk.html: Екеуі де қазірден артқа қарайды, бірақ түрлі нәрсеге назар аударады. Present perfect simple — аяқталған нәтиже/жетістік (жиі how much/how many). Present perfect continuous — әрекеттің өзі, оның ұзақтығы немесе жалғасып жатқаны (жиі how long). I've written three pages (нәтиже) vs I've been writing all afternoon (әрекет/ұзақтық).

ru.title: Форма
ru.html: Simple: have/has + past participle. Continuous: have/has + been + -ing. Оба с for/since. Simple — how many/much (завершено); continuous — how long (процесс).

kk.title: Форма
kk.html: Simple: have/has + past participle. Continuous: have/has + been + -ing. Екеуі de for/since алады. Simple — how many/much (аяқталған); continuous — how long (әрекет).

ru.title: Завершено vs ещё идёт — и видимый результат
ru.html: Continuous часто значит, что действие ещё идёт или только что прекратилось, с видимым эффектом: You're out of breath — have you been running? Simple подаёт результат как завершённый: I've cleaned the kitchen. Сравни: Who's eaten my cake? (его нет) vs Who's been eating my cake? (кто-то ел).

kk.title: Аяқталды vs әлі жүр — және көрінетін нәтиже
kk.html: Continuous жиі әрекет әлі жүріп жатыр немесе жаңа тоқтады дегенді, көрінетін әсермен: You're out of breath — have you been running? Simple нәтижені аяқталған етеді: I've cleaned the kitchen. Салыстыр: Who's eaten my cake? (жоқ болды) vs Who's been eating my cake? (біреуі жеп жатыр).

ru.title: Длительность: какой глагол что берёт
ru.html: Состояния (know, be, have=обладать, like) даже для длительности — simple: I've known her for years (не been knowing). Динамические действия до сейчас — continuous: I've been studying English since 2018. С live/work/teach оба часты, разница мала.

kk.title: Ұзақтық: қай етістік қайсын алады
kk.html: Күй етістіктері (know, be, have=иелену, like) ұзақтықта да simple: I've known her for years (been knowing емес). Динамикалық әрекет қазірге дейін жалғасса — continuous: I've been studying English since 2018. live/work/teach-пен екеуі де жиі, айырма аз.

ru.title: Нюанс: отношение и завершённость
ru.html: Continuous может подчёркивать временность или усилие/повтор: I've been trying to reach you all day! Simple даёт постоянное/полное прочтение: I've read that book (целиком) vs I've been reading that book (в процессе, может не дочитал).

kk.title: Нюанс: көзқарас пен аяқталу
kk.html: Continuous уақытшалықты немесе күш/қайталауды білдіре алады: I've been trying to reach you all day! Simple тұрақты/толық оқуды береді: I've read that book (толық) vs I've been reading that book (жүріп жатыр, әлі бітпеуі мүмкін).

ru.title: Типичные ошибки
ru.html: ✗ I've been knowing him → ✓ I've known him (состояние). ✗ I've been finishing the report (если готов) → ✓ I've finished the report. ✗ How long have you written? → если ещё идёт ✓ How long have you been writing?

kk.title: Жиі қателер
kk.html: ✗ I've been knowing him → ✓ I've known him (күй). ✗ I've been finishing the report (аяқталса) → ✓ I've finished the report. ✗ How long have you written? → әлі жүрсе ✓ How long have you been writing?

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Simple = завершённый результат/how many; continuous = длящийся процесс/how long.
  • Continuous часто = ещё идёт или только что прекратилось, с видимым эффектом.
  • Состояния для длительности — simple (have known); динамика — continuous.
  • live/work/study — оба, разница мала.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Simple = аяқталған нәтиже/how many; continuous = жалғасқан әрекет/how long.
  • Continuous жиі = әлі жүр немесе жаңа тоқтады, көрінетін әсермен.
  • Күй етістіктері ұзақтықта simple (have known); динамикалық әрекет continuous.
  • live/work/study — екеуі де, айырма аз.

activities.prompt: Sort each sentence by what it emphasises.

items.t: She's read forty books this year.
They've been arguing for an hour.

activities.why: The simple counts finished results; the continuous stresses the activity and how long it has gone on.

items.s: I've known him for ages.
I've been knowing him for ages.
How long have you been waiting?
I've been finishing my essay. (it's done)

activities.why: know is a state verb (simple only); a completed essay → I've finished it.
the ongoing activity with ‘how long/long’ → present perfect continuous Have you been waiting.
a finished, complete result → present perfect simple 've finished.
own is a state verb → present perfect simple 've owned even for duration.
a visible present effect from an ongoing activity → present perfect continuous.

activities.q: There are crumbs everywhere and half the cake is missing. Which fits?
why: the simple suggests it's all gone as a finished result — but half remains.
Correct — the messy, in-progress evidence points to the activity → ‘Who's been eating the cake?’
the past simple loses the ‘look at the mess now’ connection to the present.

activities.q: “You've done so well! ___ have you ___ so far?”
why: ‘how long’ + been reading asks about duration, not the countable result.
Correct — a countable, completed amount → simple: ‘How many pages have you read?’
this mixes ‘how long’ with the simple oddly.

activities.prompt: For each verb, which perfect form is natural with a duration?
activities.why: State verbs (believe, belong) use the simple; dynamic activities (wait, run) take the continuous for duration.
‘have’ meaning possession is a state verb → present perfect simple “I have had this laptop for three years.” (delete ‘been … having’)

activities.instruction: Rewrite to stress the ongoing effort (still not successful):
activities.prompt: I couldn't reach you all day.
activities.answer: i've been trying to reach you all day
alts: i have been trying to reach you all day
activities.why: repeated, ongoing effort up to now → present perfect continuous 've been trying.

activities.instruction: Rewrite to stress a finished, countable result:
activities.prompt: She's been sending emails all morning. (state the total)
activities.answer: she's sent twenty emails this morning
alts: she has sent twenty emails this morning
she's sent dozens of emails this morning
activities.why: a completed count → present perfect simple 's sent + a number.

activities.scene: Choose result (simple) or activity (continuous).

steps.npc: You seem exhausted — what have you been up to?

options.t: I've been decorating all day, and I've finally finished the living room.
options.why: ongoing activity (been decorating) + completed result (finished).

options.t: I've decorated all day, and I've been finishing the living room.
options.why: the all-day activity → been decorating; the done room → finished.

steps.npc: How long have you owned the place?

options.t: We've had it for two years, and we've been renovating it ever since.
options.why: state ‘have had’ + ongoing ‘been renovating’.

options.t: We've been having it for two years, and we've renovated it ever since.
options.why: ‘have’ (possess) is a state → ‘we've had it’; the ongoing work → ‘we've been renovating’.

activities.why: Use the present perfect simple for completed, countable results and the continuous for ongoing activity and duration — keeping state verbs in the simple.

learn.title: The perfect aspect, shifted into the past
learn.html:
The past perfect is simply the present perfect moved one step back. Where the present perfect looks back from now, the past perfect looks back from a point in the past.

now: She has left.a past point: By 6, she had left.
now: I've been waiting an hour.a past point: By then I'd been waiting an hour.

So all the perfect-aspect meanings — result, completion-before, duration up to a point — carry over, but the reference point is earlier.

learn.title: Form
learn.html:
Past perfect simple: had + past participle (had gone, had seen). Past perfect continuous: had been + -ing (had been working). The contracted 'd is common: She'd finished; they'd been travelling.

Negatives/questions: hadn't left · Had you met before?

learn.title: Simple: completion & result before a past moment
learn.html:
The simple presents an event as complete before the past reference point, often with a result then:

  • The train had already left when we got to the platform.
  • She was nervous because she hadn't flown before.
  • By the age of ten he had read the whole series.

learn.title: Continuous: activity & duration up to a past moment
learn.html:
The continuous stresses the ongoing activity or its duration leading up to the past point — frequently explaining a past state or appearance:

  • The ground was wet; it had been raining.
  • She was fluent because she had been studying for years.
  • By midnight we had been driving for eight hours.

learn.title: Choosing simple vs continuous
learn.html:
He had painted the fence.finished — the fence was done → simple
He had been painting the fence.that's why he had paint on his hands → continuous

As with the present perfect, use the simple for a completed result or amount, and the continuous for the process/duration. State verbs stay simple: I'd known her for years (not had been knowing).

learn.title: Typical learner errors & register
learn.html:
✗ By then I had been finishing the work. (if complete) → ✓ By then I had finished the work.

✗ He had been owning the shop for years. → ✓ He had owned the shop for years. (state verb)

The past perfect belongs in contexts with a clear past reference point (a story, a report, a ‘by then’). In simple chronological sequence it's usually unnecessary.

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• Past perfect = the perfect aspect seen from a past reference point.
  • Simple (had done) = completed/result before then; continuous (had been doing) = ongoing activity/duration up to then.
  • Use the continuous to explain a past state (wet ground, tired eyes).
  • State verbs use the simple (had known), never the continuous.

ru.title: Перфектный аспект, сдвинутый в прошлое
ru.html: Past perfect — это present perfect, сдвинутый на шаг назад. Present perfect смотрит назад из сейчас, past perfect — из точки в прошлом: now She has left → a past point By 6, she had left. Все перфектные значения (результат, завершённость раньше, длительность до момента) сохраняются, но точка отсчёта раньше.

kk.title: Perfect аспект — өткенге ауысқан
kk.html: Past perfect — present perfect-тің бір қадам артқа жылжығаны. Present perfect қазірден артқа қараса, past perfect өткендегі нүктеден артқа қарайды: now She has left → a past point By 6, she had left. Барлық perfect мағыналары (нәтиже, бұрын аяқталу, нүктеге дейінгі ұзақтық) сақталады, тек тірек нүкте ертерек.

ru.title: Форма
ru.html: Past perfect simple: had + past participle. Past perfect continuous: had been + -ing. Сокращённое 'd часто: She'd finished; they'd been travelling. Отриц./вопрос: hadn't left · Had you met before?

kk.title: Форма
kk.html: Past perfect simple: had + past participle. Past perfect continuous: had been + -ing. Қысқарған 'd жиі: She'd finished; they'd been travelling. Болымсыз/сұрақ: hadn't left · Had you met before?

ru.title: Simple: завершённость/результат до прошлого момента
ru.html: Simple подаёт событие как завершённое до прошлой точки отсчёта, часто с результатом тогда: The train had already left when we got to the platform. She was nervous because she hadn't flown before. By ten he had read the whole series.

kk.title: Simple: өткен сәтке дейінгі аяқталу/нәтиже
kk.html: Simple оқиғаны өткен тірек нүктеге дейін аяқталған етеді, жиі сол кездегі нәтижемен: The train had already left when we got to the platform. She was nervous because she hadn't flown before. By ten he had read the whole series.

ru.title: Continuous: процесс/длительность до прошлого момента
ru.html: Continuous подчёркивает длящееся действие/длительность до прошлой точки, часто объясняя прошлое состояние: The ground was wet; it had been raining. She was fluent because she had been studying for years. By midnight we had been driving for eight hours.

kk.title: Continuous: өткен сәтке дейінгі әрекет/ұзақтық
kk.html: Continuous өткен нүктеге дейінгі жалғасқан әрекет/ұзақтықты баса көрсетеді, жиі өткендегі күйді түсіндіреді: The ground was wet; it had been raining. She was fluent because she had been studying for years. By midnight we had been driving for eight hours.

ru.title: Выбор simple vs continuous
ru.html: He had painted the fence (завершено — забор готов → simple) vs He had been painting the fence (потому руки в краске → continuous). Как с present perfect: завершённый результат/количество → simple; процесс/длительность → continuous. Состояния — simple: I'd known her for years (не had been knowing).

kk.title: Simple vs continuous таңдау
kk.html: He had painted the fence (аяқталды — қоршау дайын → simple) vs He had been painting the fence (қолы бояуда болды → continuous). Present perfect сияқты: аяқталған нәтиже/мөлшер → simple; үдеріс/ұзақтық → continuous. Күй етістіктері simple: I'd known her for years (had been knowing емес).

ru.title: Типичные ошибки и регистр
ru.html: ✗ By then I had been finishing the work (если завершено) → ✓ I had finished the work. ✗ He had been owning the shop → ✓ He had owned the shop (состояние). Past perfect уместен там, где есть ясная прошлая точка отсчёта (рассказ, отчёт, ‘by then’); в простой последовательности обычно не нужен.

kk.title: Жиі қателер мен регистр
kk.html: ✗ By then I had been finishing the work (аяқталса) → ✓ I had finished the work. ✗ He had been owning the shop → ✓ He had owned the shop (күй). Past perfect анық өткен тірек нүкте бар жерде (әңгіме, есеп, ‘by then’) қолданылады; жай реттік тізбекте әдетте қажет емес.

ru.title: Кратко
ru.html:
• Past perfect = перфектный аспект из прошлой точки отсчёта.
  • Simple (had done) = завершённость/результат до тех пор; continuous (had been doing) = процесс/длительность до тех пор.
  • Для объяснения прошлого состояния — continuous (мокрая земля, усталые глаза).
  • Состояния — simple (had known), не continuous.

kk.title: Қысқаша
kk.html:
• Past perfect = өткен тірек нүктеден көрінген perfect аспект.
  • Simple (had done) = сол кезге дейін аяқталу/нәтиже; continuous (had been doing) = сол кезге дейінгі әрекет/ұзақтық.
  • Өткен күйді түсіндіру үшін continuous (жаңбырлы жер, шаршаған көз).
  • Күй етістіктері simple (had known), continuous емес.

activities.prompt: Sort each past-perfect form by what it emphasises.

items.t: had been trying to sell it

activities.why: The simple marks completion before the past point; the continuous marks the activity/duration leading up to it.

items.s: She was tired because she'd been working late.
By 9 the shop had closed.
I'd been knowing them for years.
By then I had been finishing the project. (it was done)

activities.why: know is a state verb (had known); a completed project → had finished.

activities.after: for two hours in the heat. (train)
activities.why: an ongoing activity explaining their past state → past perfect continuous had been training.

activities.before: By the time the guests arrived, she
activities.why: a completed result before their arrival → past perfect simple had already prepared.

activities.after: online for months. (talk — a state-like continuous relationship? no: use dynamic)
activities.why: talk is dynamic and ongoing up to that point → past perfect continuous had been talking.
the continuous explains the past state of the road.

activities.q: “His shirt was soaked. He ___ in the rain to catch us.”
why: the simple would stress the completed act, not the visible dripping-wet result.
Correct — the process explaining his soaked shirt → past perfect continuous had been running.

activities.q: Report in the past: “I have lived here for ten years,” she said. → She said she ___ there for ten years.
why: the present perfect shifts back in reported speech.
Correct — past perfect had lived (the perfect aspect moved one step back).
the past simple loses the ‘up to that point’ meaning.

activities.typeLabel: Which past-perfect form fits the verb?
activities.prompt: For each verb + duration up to a past point, which form is natural?
activities.why: State verbs use the simple (had known/believed); dynamic activities use the continuous (had been waiting/driving).
reaching a summit is a completed achievement → past perfect simple “we had reached the summit.” (delete ‘been … reaching’)

activities.instruction: Combine, explaining the past state with a continuous form:
activities.prompt: Her eyes were red. She had cried for hours.
activities.answer: her eyes were red because she had been crying for hours
alts: her eyes were red because she'd been crying for hours
activities.why: the ongoing prior activity explains the state → past perfect continuous had been crying.

activities.instruction: Rewrite so the earlier event is complete before the past point:
activities.prompt: We got to the cinema. The film started before that.
activities.answer: the film had already started when we got to the cinema
alts: by the time we got to the cinema, the film had started
the film had started before we got to the cinema
activities.why: a completed event before a past reference point → past perfect simple had (already) started.

activities.typeLabel: Extended context: piecing together an evening
activities.scene: Choose the perfect form that fits.

steps.npc: Why was the kitchen such a mess when you got home?

options.t: The kids had been baking, and they hadn't cleaned up.
options.why: ongoing activity (had been baking) + completed non-action (hadn't cleaned).

options.t: The kids had baked, and they hadn't been cleaning up.
options.why: the mess points to the ongoing activity (had been baking); the failure to tidy is a completed fact (hadn't cleaned up).

steps.npc: Were they still up?

options.t: No — by then they'd fallen asleep. They'd been playing outside all day.
options.why: completed ‘had fallen asleep’ + ongoing ‘had been playing’ up to that point.

options.t: No — by then they'd been falling asleep. They'd played outside all day.
options.why: falling asleep is a completed change → had fallen; the day-long activity → had been playing.

activities.why: The past perfect is the perfect aspect seen from a past point: use the simple for completion/result before then and the continuous for ongoing activity or duration up to then — keeping state verbs in the simple.

learn.title: English has no single 'future tense'
learn.html:
English expresses the future with several structures, and the choice reflects how the speaker sees the event — a prediction, a plan, an arrangement, a schedule, or a decision made now.

  • will — predictions, instant decisions, offers, promises
  • going to — intentions & evidence-based predictions
  • present continuous — fixed arrangements
  • present simple — timetables & schedules

learn.title: will vs going to
learn.html:
I think it'll rain later.a prediction / opinion → will
Look at those clouds — it's going to rain.a prediction from present evidence → going to
— The phone's ringing. — I'll get it.a decision made at the moment → will
We're going to paint the house.a plan/intention decided before → going to

learn.title: present continuous & present simple
learn.html:
Present continuous = a fixed personal arrangement, usually with another person and often a time/place: I'm meeting Sam at six. · We're flying to Rome on Friday.

Present simple = a timetable/schedule fixed by an institution: The train leaves at nine. · The exam starts on Monday.

Compare: I'm seeing the dentist tomorrow (my arrangement) vs The surgery opens at eight (its timetable).

learn.title: going to vs present continuous
learn.html:
Both express future plans, but present continuous suggests a more definite arrangement (details settled), while going to can express an intention not yet arranged: I'm going to learn Italian (intention) vs I'm starting Italian classes on Monday (arranged).

With verbs of movement (go, come, leave), the present continuous is preferred over going to go: We're going to Spain (not usually going to go).

learn.title: After when / if / as soon as — present, not will
learn.html:
In time and conditional clauses about the future, use the present, not will: I'll call you when I arrive (not when I will arrive). If it rains, we'll stay in.

This applies after when, as soon as, before, after, until, if, unless.

learn.title: Register & shall
learn.html:
shall survives mainly in suggestions/offers (I/we): Shall we go? · Shall I help? and in formal/legal rules (The tenant shall pay…). For plain predictions, will is standard.

be going to is slightly more informal/spoken; will and the present forms are used across registers.

learn.title: Typical learner errors
learn.html:
✗ I will meet Sam tomorrow at six. (fixed arrangement) → ✓ I'm meeting Sam tomorrow at six.

✗ When I will finish, I'll call. → ✓ When I finish, I'll call.

✗ Look — it will rain! (evidence now) → ✓ Look — it's going to rain!

learn.title: In a nutshell
learn.html:
• will = prediction / instant decision / offer / promise.
  • going to = intention & evidence-based prediction.
  • present continuous = fixed arrangement; present simple = timetable.
  • After when/if/as soon as → present tense, not will.

ru.title: В английском нет единого «будущего времени»
ru.html: Будущее выражается несколькими структурами, и выбор отражает как говорящий видит событие: will — прогноз, мгновенное решение, предложение, обещание; going to — намерение и прогноз по признакам; present continuous — договорённость; present simple — расписание.
